planting.note: Direct sow in spring in dry, sandy, acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun; Gulf bluestem is a coastal specialist growing in dry, sandy, coastal habitats from Texas to North Carolina. It is one of the most important native grasses for beach and dune stabilization on Gulf Coast shorelines.

directions: Plant in dry, sandy, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun along coastal dunes, sandy beaches, and dry, exposed coastal garden settings; Gulf bluestem is a low-growing, creeping-rooted native bluestem of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain, especially adapted to the harsh, salt-spray, wind-exposed, nutrient-poor sandy soils of coastal dunes and beach strands. Unlike upland bluestems that form upright bunches, this species has a more sprawling, prostrate habit with shorter culms and broad, glaucous blue-green leaves that withstand the physical stress of coastal exposure. The fine seed heads in fall provide food for coastal sparrows and other birds. An outstanding native for beach and dune gardens in zone 9a where it stabilizes sandy areas with minimal care. Spreads slowly by short rhizomes to form low, dense mats. More salt-tolerant than most native grasses and naturally resistant to wind and drought in coastal conditions.

planting.note: Direct sow or divide established clumps in spring in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; silver plumegrass grows naturally in moist, open areas, stream banks, and forest margins across the southeastern US.

directions: Plant in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; silver plumegrass is a native tall grass of the southeastern Coastal Plain producing spectacular, large, feathery, silvery-white plume seed heads in fall that shimmer in the breeze — one of the most ornamentally dramatic of all native grasses. The plumes are produced on tall, robust culms reaching 5–10 feet, creating a bold, upright architectural statement in the late-summer and fall landscape. The silky-silver plumes are as showy as those of ornamental miscanthus but on a native plant that supports native wildlife — the seeds provide food for numerous sparrows and finches, and the dense clumps provide nesting habitat. A superb bold specimen grass for moist roadsides, stream banks, naturalistic borders, and the back of large native plantings in zones 5–9. The plumes persist through winter, providing long-lasting seasonal interest.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; forked bluestem grows naturally in moist to wet pine flatwoods, seasonal wetland edges, and disturbed moist areas of the Gulf Coastal Plain.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; forked bluestem is a distinctive tall native grass of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain, named for the unusually forked arrangement of its seed-bearing spikes — unlike most bluestems with single spikes, this species produces multiple racemes at the stem tips that fan out in a fan-like or V-forked pattern, creating a bird-foot effect at the top of each stem. The silky seed heads are covered in long, gossamer hairs that catch the light and shimmer silver-white in fall sun. It grows naturally in moist to wet pine flatwoods, the margins of bay swamps, and disturbed moist roadsides throughout the Gulf South, tolerating seasonal flooding better than most bluestems. The fall foliage turns orange-copper to burgundy, combining dramatically with the silver-white seed heads. An underused native grass for moist sites in zone 9a where it provides bold autumn color and distinctive textural interest.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to alkaline, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; silver beardgrass grows naturally on dry, rocky prairies, limestone outcrops, and dry hillsides across the south-central US. Drought-tolerant once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, lean, slightly acidic to neutral or alkaline, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; silver beardgrass is a native grass of dry prairies, limestone outcrops, and rocky hillsides across the south-central United States, particularly abundant on dry, calcareous soils of the Edwards Plateau and Texas Hill Country. The plant produces an unusually showy display for a grass — the seed heads are covered in long, bright white hairs that create a shimmering silver effect across the entire upper portion of the plant in summer, making it appear as if the grass is in bloom with silver flowers. The effect is particularly dramatic when mass planted or when backlit by afternoon sun. Extremely drought and heat tolerant once established; thrives in the limestone-derived, thin, rocky soils typical of Texas rangelands. An outstanding ornamental native grass for dry, sunny gardens and prairies in zones 5–10.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; palmarosa grass grows in tropical and subtropical conditions and is killed by frost. In zone 9, it may be grown as a warm-season annual or perennial in frost-free microclimates.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in frost-free zones 10–12; palmarosa grass is an Indian aromatic grass in the same genus as lemongrass and citronella, cultivated throughout tropical Asia and Madagascar for its essential oil — one of the most widely used in perfumery. The essential oil, distilled from the leaves and tops, is dominated by geraniol (70–85%), giving it a sweet, rose-like fragrance with floral and earthy undertones. It is used extensively in cosmetics, soaps, food flavorings, and aromatherapy and is one of the most popular natural geraniol sources in the fragrance industry. The grass has traditionally been used to adulterate or substitute for expensive attar of roses in Indian perfumery — a use reflected in the common name. In the home garden, it provides an attractive clumping grass that can be harvested by cutting back and steam-distilling the biomass for aromatic purposes.
